frameworks.name: self-consistent Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: decoder that jointly recovers ordinal and absolute stimulus features under one posterior

frameworks.name: hierarchical high-to-low decoding
frameworks.one_line: ordinal category constrains subsequent absolute-orientation recall

core_question: Does decoding high-level ordinal information before low-level absolute features actually improve perceptual performance?
key_assumptions: observer follows a self-consistent Bayesian posterior across feature levels
ordinal and absolute orientation are encoded from a shared noisy sensory signal
manipulations: noise level on the encoded sensory signal
signal conditions defining the ordinal relation between two lines
key_findings: high-to-low decoding does not generally outperform direct decoding of absolute orientation
any performance benefit depends narrowly on specific noise and signal regimes
interpretations: Ding et al.'s claim that the brain adopts this strategy because it improves performance is misleading
the proposed scheme is consistent with a self-consistent Bayesian observer but not justified by accuracy gains
